question:
Problem B-3
Let \( p_{n} \) be the probability that \( c+d \) is a perfect square when the integers \( c \) and \( d \) are selected independently at random from the set \( \{1,2,3, \ldots, n\} \). Show that \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(p_{n} \sqrt{n}\right) \) exists and express this limit in the form \( r(\sqrt{s}-1) \). where \( s \) and \( t \) are integers and \( r \) is a rational number.
solution:
B-3.
Let \( a(n)=[\sqrt{n+1}] \) and \( b(n)=[\sqrt{2 n}] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). For \( t \) in \( \{1,2, \ldots, a(n)\} \), there are \( t^{2}-1 \) ordered pairs \( (c, d) \) with \( c \) and \( d \) in \( X=\{1,2, \ldots, n\} \) and \( c+d=t^{2} \). For \( t \) in \( \{1+a(n), 2+a(n), \ldots, b(n)\} \), there are \( 2 n+1-t^{2} \) ordered pairs \( (c, d) \) with \( c \) and \( d \) in \( X \) and \( c+d=t^{2} \). Hence the total number \( F(n) \) of favorable \( (c, d) \) is
\[
\begin{aligned}
F(n)= & \sum_{t=1}^{a(n)}\left(t^{2}-1\right)+\sum_{t=1+a(n)}^{b(n)}\left(2 n+1-t^{2}\right) \\
= & \left(2 \sum_{t=1}^{a(n)} t^{2}\right)-\left(\sum_{t=1}^{b(n)} t^{2}\right)-a(n)+[b(n)-a(n)](2 n+1) \\
= & \frac{2 a(n)[1+a(n)][1+2 a(n)]}{6}-\frac{b(n)[1+b(n)][1+2 b(n)]}{6} \\
& -2(n+1) a(n)+(2 n+1) b(n) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( p_{n}=F(n) / n^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(p_{n} \sqrt{n}\right) & =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} F(n) / n^{3 / 2} \\
& =\frac{2 \cdot 2}{6} \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{a(n)}{\sqrt{n}}\right)^{3}-\frac{2}{6} \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{b(n)}{\sqrt{n}}\right)^{3}-2 \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{a(n)}{\sqrt{n}}+2 \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{b(n)}{\sqrt{n}} \\
& =\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{3}(\sqrt{2})^{3}-2+2 \sqrt{2}=\frac{4}{3}(\sqrt{2}-1)
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-3
Let \( squareprob \) be the probability that \( firstint+secondint \) is a perfect square when the integers \( firstint \) and \( secondint \) are selected independently at random from the set \( \{1,2,3, \ldots, maxvalue\} \). Show that \( \lim _{maxvalue \rightarrow \infty}\left(squareprob \sqrt{maxvalue}\right) \) exists and express this limit in the form \( ratioscale(\sqrt{basesquare}-1) \). where \( basesquare \) and \( rootsize \) are integers and \( ratioscale \) is a rational number.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-3.
Let \( firstfloor(maxvalue)=[\sqrt{maxvalue+1}] \) and \( secondfloor(maxvalue)=[\sqrt{2\, maxvalue}] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). For \( rootsize \) in \( \{1,2, \ldots, firstfloor(maxvalue)\} \), there are \( rootsize^{2}-1 \) ordered pairs \( (firstint, secondint) \) with \( firstint \) and \( secondint \) in \( integerset=\{1,2, \ldots, maxvalue\} \) and \( firstint+secondint=rootsize^{2} \). For \( rootsize \) in \( \{1+firstfloor(maxvalue), 2+firstfloor(maxvalue), \ldots, secondfloor(maxvalue)\} \), there are \( 2\, maxvalue+1-rootsize^{2} \) ordered pairs \( (firstint, secondint) \) with \( firstint \) and \( secondint \) in \( integerset \) and \( firstint+secondint=rootsize^{2} \). Hence the total number \( totalfav(maxvalue) \) of favorable \( (firstint, secondint) \) is
\[
\begin{aligned}
totalfav(maxvalue)= & \sum_{rootsize=1}^{firstfloor(maxvalue)}\left(rootsize^{2}-1\right)+\sum_{rootsize=1+firstfloor(maxvalue)}^{secondfloor(maxvalue)}\left(2\, maxvalue+1-rootsize^{2}\right) \\
= & \left(2 \sum_{rootsize=1}^{firstfloor(maxvalue)} rootsize^{2}\right)-\left(\sum_{rootsize=1}^{secondfloor(maxvalue)} rootsize^{2}\right)-firstfloor(maxvalue)+[secondfloor(maxvalue)-firstfloor(maxvalue)](2\, maxvalue+1) \\
= & \frac{2\, firstfloor(maxvalue)[1+firstfloor(maxvalue)][1+2\, firstfloor(maxvalue)]}{6}-\frac{secondfloor(maxvalue)[1+secondfloor(maxvalue)][1+2\, secondfloor(maxvalue)]}{6} \\
& -2(maxvalue+1)\, firstfloor(maxvalue)+(2\, maxvalue+1)\, secondfloor(maxvalue) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( squareprob=totalfav(maxvalue) / maxvalue^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim _{maxvalue \rightarrow \infty}\left(squareprob \sqrt{maxvalue}\right) & =\lim _{maxvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{totalfav(maxvalue)}{maxvalue^{3 / 2}} \\
& =\frac{2 \cdot 2}{6} \lim _{maxvalue \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{firstfloor(maxvalue)}{\sqrt{maxvalue}}\right)^{3}-\frac{2}{6} \lim _{maxvalue \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{secondfloor(maxvalue)}{\sqrt{maxvalue}}\right)^{3}-2 \lim _{maxvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{firstfloor(maxvalue)}{\sqrt{maxvalue}}+2 \lim _{maxvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{secondfloor(maxvalue)}{\sqrt{maxvalue}} \\
& =\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{3}(\sqrt{2})^{3}-2+2 \sqrt{2}=\frac{4}{3}(\sqrt{2}-1)
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-3
Let \( gastronom_{snowflake} \) be the probability that \( lanterns+notebook \) is a perfect square when the integers \( lanterns \) and \( notebook \) are selected independently at random from the set \{1,2,3, \ldots, snowflake\}. Show that \( \lim _{snowflake \rightarrow \infty}\left(gastronom_{snowflake} \sqrt{snowflake}\right) \) exists and express this limit in the form \( tangerine(\sqrt{waterfall}-1) \). where \( waterfall \) and \( buttercup \) are integers and \( tangerine \) is a rational number.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-3.
Let \( pendulum(snowflake)=[\sqrt{snowflake+1}] \) and \( marigold(snowflake)=[\sqrt{2 snowflake}] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). For \( buttercup \) in \{1,2, \ldots, pendulum(snowflake)\}, there are \( buttercup^{2}-1 \) ordered pairs \( (lanterns, notebook) \) with \( lanterns \) and \( notebook \) in \( silhouette=\{1,2, \ldots, snowflake\} \) and \( lanterns+notebook=buttercup^{2} \). For \( buttercup \) in \{1+pendulum(snowflake), 2+pendulum(snowflake), \ldots, marigold(snowflake)\}, there are \( 2 snowflake+1-buttercup^{2} \) ordered pairs \( (lanterns, notebook) \) with \( lanterns \) and \( notebook \) in \( silhouette \) and \( lanterns+notebook=buttercup^{2} \). Hence the total number \( harmonica(snowflake) \) of favorable \( (lanterns, notebook) \) is
\[
\begin{aligned}
harmonica(snowflake)= & \sum_{buttercup=1}^{pendulum(snowflake)}\left(buttercup^{2}-1\right)+\sum_{buttercup=1+pendulum(snowflake)}^{marigold(snowflake)}\left(2 snowflake+1-buttercup^{2}\right) \\
= & \left(2 \sum_{buttercup=1}^{pendulum(snowflake)} buttercup^{2}\right)-\left(\sum_{buttercup=1}^{marigold(snowflake)} buttercup^{2}\right)-pendulum(snowflake)+[marigold(snowflake)-pendulum(snowflake)](2 snowflake+1) \\
= & \frac{2 pendulum(snowflake)[1+pendulum(snowflake)][1+2 pendulum(snowflake)]}{6}-\frac{marigold(snowflake)[1+marigold(snowflake)][1+2 marigold(snowflake)]}{6} \\
& -2(snowflake+1) pendulum(snowflake)+(2 snowflake+1) marigold(snowflake) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( gastronom_{snowflake}=harmonica(snowflake) / snowflake^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim _{snowflake \rightarrow \infty}\left(gastronom_{snowflake} \sqrt{snowflake}\right) & =\lim _{snowflake \rightarrow \infty} harmonica(snowflake) / snowflake^{3 / 2} \\
& =\frac{2 \cdot 2}{6} \lim _{snowflake \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{pendulum(snowflake)}{\sqrt{snowflake}}\right)^{3}-\frac{2}{6} \lim _{snowflake \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{marigold(snowflake)}{\sqrt{snowflake}}\right)^{3}-2 \lim _{snowflake \rightarrow \infty} \frac{pendulum(snowflake)}{\sqrt{snowflake}}+2 \lim _{snowflake \rightarrow \infty} \frac{marigold(snowflake)}{\sqrt{snowflake}} \\
& =\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{3}(\sqrt{2})^{3}-2+2 \sqrt{2}=\frac{4}{3}(\sqrt{2}-1)
\end{aligned}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-3
Let \( improbability \) be the probability that \( floatvalue+fractionalvalue \) is a perfect square when the integers \( floatvalue \) and \( fractionalvalue \) are selected independently at random from the set \( \{1,2,3, \ldots, unlimitedsize\} \). Show that \( \lim _{unlimitedsize \rightarrow \infty}\left(improbability \sqrt{unlimitedsize}\right) \) exists and express this limit in the form \( irrationalnum(\sqrt{nonsquarevalue}-1) \). where \( nonsquarevalue \) and \( stillnessvalue \) are integers and \( irrationalnum \) is a rational number.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-3.
Let \( cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)=[\sqrt{unlimitedsize+1}] \) and \( apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)=[\sqrt{2 unlimitedsize}] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). For \( stillnessvalue \) in \( \{1,2, \ldots, cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)\} \), there are \( stillnessvalue^{2}-1 \) ordered pairs \( (floatvalue, fractionalvalue) \) with \( floatvalue \) and \( fractionalvalue \) in \( complementset=\{1,2, \ldots, unlimitedsize\} \) and \( floatvalue+fractionalvalue=stillnessvalue^{2} \). For \( stillnessvalue \) in \( \{1+cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize), 2+cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize), \ldots, apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)\} \), there are \( 2 unlimitedsize+1-stillnessvalue^{2} \) ordered pairs \( (floatvalue, fractionalvalue) \) with \( floatvalue \) and \( fractionalvalue \) in \( complementset \) and \( floatvalue+fractionalvalue=stillnessvalue^{2} \). Hence the total number \( unfavorablecount(unlimitedsize) \) of favorable \( (floatvalue, fractionalvalue) \) is
\[
\begin{aligned}
unfavorablecount(unlimitedsize)= & \sum_{stillnessvalue=1}^{cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)}\left(stillnessvalue^{2}-1\right)+\sum_{stillnessvalue=1+cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)}^{apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)}\left(2 unlimitedsize+1-stillnessvalue^{2}\right) \\
= & \left(2 \sum_{stillnessvalue=1}^{cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)} stillnessvalue^{2}\right)-\left(\sum_{stillnessvalue=1}^{apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)} stillnessvalue^{2}\right)-cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)+[apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)-cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)](2 unlimitedsize+1) \\
= & \frac{2 cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)[1+cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)][1+2 cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)]}{6}-\frac{apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)[1+apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)][1+2 apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)]}{6} \\
& -2(unlimitedsize+1) cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)+(2 unlimitedsize+1) apexmeasure(unlimitedsize) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( improbability=unfavorablecount(unlimitedsize) / unlimitedsize^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim _{unlimitedsize \rightarrow \infty}\left(improbability \sqrt{unlimitedsize}\right) & =\lim _{unlimitedsize \rightarrow \infty} unfavorablecount(unlimitedsize) / unlimitedsize^{3 / 2} \\
& =\frac{2 \cdot 2}{6} \lim _{unlimitedsize \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)}{\sqrt{unlimitedsize}}\right)^{3}-\frac{2}{6} \lim _{unlimitedsize \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)}{\sqrt{unlimitedsize}}\right)^{3}-2 \lim _{unlimitedsize \rightarrow \infty} \frac{cubicmeasure(unlimitedsize)}{\sqrt{unlimitedsize}}+2 \lim _{unlimitedsize \rightarrow \infty} \frac{apexmeasure(unlimitedsize)}{\sqrt{unlimitedsize}} \\
& =\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{3}(\sqrt{2})^{3}-2+2 \sqrt{2}=\frac{4}{3}(\sqrt{2}-1)
\end{aligned}
\]

garbled_string.question:
Problem B-3
Let \( qzxwvtnp \) be the probability that \( hjgrksla+mnlpqrst \) is a perfect square when the integers \( hjgrksla \) and \( mnlpqrst \) are selected independently at random from the set \( \{1,2,3, \ldots, gnbshplt\} \). Show that \( \lim _{gnbshplt \rightarrow \infty}\left(qzxwvtnp \sqrt{gnbshplt}\right) \) exists and express this limit in the form \( xckptfma(\sqrt{qrlgnpzw}-1) \). where \( qrlgnpzw \) and \( vrwzcpky \) are integers and \( xckptfma \) is a rational number.
garbled_string.solution:
B-3.
Let \( slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)=[\sqrt{gnbshplt+1}] \) and \( kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)=[\sqrt{2 gnbshplt}] \), where \( [x] \) is the greatest integer in \( x \). For \( vrwzcpky \) in \( \{1,2, \ldots, slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)\} \), there are \( vrwzcpky^{2}-1 \) ordered pairs \( (hjgrksla, mnlpqrst) \) with \( hjgrksla \) and \( mnlpqrst \) in \( zbfruoyg=\{1,2, \ldots, gnbshplt\} \) and \( hjgrksla+mnlpqrst=vrwzcpky^{2} \). For \( vrwzcpky \) in \( \{1+slqzmhvd(gnbshplt), 2+slqzmhvd(gnbshplt), \ldots, kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)\} \), there are \( 2 gnbshplt+1-vrwzcpky^{2} \) ordered pairs \( (hjgrksla, mnlpqrst) \) with \( hjgrksla \) and \( mnlpqrst \) in \( zbfruoyg \) and \( hjgrksla+mnlpqrst=vrwzcpky^{2} \). Hence the total number \( wldjskrp(gnbshplt) \) of favorable \( (hjgrksla, mnlpqrst) \) is
\[
\begin{aligned}
wldjskrp(gnbshplt)= & \sum_{vrwzcpky=1}^{slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)}\left(vrwzcpky^{2}-1\right)+\sum_{vrwzcpky=1+slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)}^{kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)}\left(2 gnbshplt+1-vrwzcpky^{2}\right) \\
= & \left(2 \sum_{vrwzcpky=1}^{slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)} vrwzcpky^{2}\right)-\left(\sum_{vrwzcpky=1}^{kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)} vrwzcpky^{2}\right)-slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)+[kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)-slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)](2 gnbshplt+1) \\
= & \frac{2\, slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)[1+slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)][1+2\, slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)]}{6}-\frac{kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)[1+kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)][1+2\, kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)]}{6} \\
& -2(gnbshplt+1)\, slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)+(2 gnbshplt+1)\, kjptwqxr(gnbshplt) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \( qzxwvtnp=wldjskrp(gnbshplt) / gnbshplt^{2} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim _{gnbshplt \rightarrow \infty}\left(qzxwvtnp \sqrt{gnbshplt}\right) & =\lim _{gnbshplt \rightarrow \infty} wldjskrp(gnbshplt) / gnbshplt^{3 / 2} \\
& =\frac{2 \cdot 2}{6} \lim _{gnbshplt \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)}{\sqrt{gnbshplt}}\right)^{3}-\frac{2}{6} \lim _{gnbshplt \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)}{\sqrt{gnbshplt}}\right)^{3}-2 \lim _{gnbshplt \rightarrow \infty} \frac{slqzmhvd(gnbshplt)}{\sqrt{gnbshplt}}+2 \lim _{gnbshplt \rightarrow \infty} \frac{kjptwqxr(gnbshplt)}{\sqrt{gnbshplt}} \\
& =\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{3}(\sqrt{2})^{3}-2+2 \sqrt{2}=\frac{4}{3}(\sqrt{2}-1)
\end{aligned}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an odd positive integer  
  m  (so that 2 is invertible modulo m).

For every n\in \mathbb{N} put  
  X_n := {5,6,7,\ldots ,n+4}.

Choose two integers c and d independently and uniformly at random from X_n and define  

  p_n := P[(A) c+d is a perfect square,  
      (B) c \equiv  d (mod m), and  
      (C) c and d have opposite parity].

Prove that the limit  

  L(m) := lim_{n\to \infty } (p_n \sqrt{n})

exists and that  

  L(m) = (2 / 3m)\cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1).

(For m = 1 we recover the classical constant (2/3)(\sqrt{2}-1); the modular restriction divides the constant by m, while the parity requirement removes the factor 2 that appears in the AIME 1994 problem.)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout ``pair'' means ordered pair (c,d); all implied constants are absolute.

0.  Basic reformulation  
Let  

  F(n) := # {(c,d)\in X_n^2 : (A), (B), (C) hold}.                      (1)

Because |X_n| = n,  

  p_n = F(n)/n^2  \Rightarrow   p_n\sqrt{n} = F(n)/n^{3/2}.                    (2)

Hence we need an asymptotic expansion for F(n).


1.  Pairs whose sum is a square after the +4 shift  
Write  

  S(n) := # {(c,d)\in X_n^2 : c+d is a perfect square}.             (3)

Denote, for 1\leq s\leq 2n,  

  R_n(s) := # {(u,v)\in [1,n]^2 : u+v=s}  
       = max(0, min(s-1, 2n+1-s)).                       (4)

For X_n the translation (c,d)\mapsto (c-4,d-4) sends X_n^2 bijectively onto [1,n]^2, and

  # {(c,d)\in X_n^2 : c+d=s} = R_n(s-8).                            (5)

Hence, if s_k:=k^2,

  S(n)=\sum _{k\geq 1} R_n(s_k-8).                                     (6)

Classically (AIME 1994)  

  S_0(n):=\sum _{k\geq 1} R_n(s_k)= (4/3)(\sqrt{2}-1) n^{3/2}+O(n).         (7)

We compare S(n) and S_0(n).

Fix k with s_k\leq 2n+8.  If both s_k and s_k-8 lie in [2,2n] then, because R_n is 1-Lipschitz,  

  |R_n(s_k-8)-R_n(s_k)|\leq 8.                                (8)

There are \lfloor \sqrt{2n+8}\rfloor  terms, giving a contribution O(\sqrt{n}).

If exactly one of s_k, s_k-8 lies in [2,2n], then s_k is within 8 of an endpoint of that interval; at most 16 such k occur, each contributing \leq n.  Therefore

  |S(n)-S_0(n)| = O(n).                                     (9)

Inserting (7) we obtain  

  S(n)= (4/3)(\sqrt{2}-1) n^{3/2}+O(n).                        (10)


2.  Keeping only odd squares - parity filter (C)  
Let  

  S_odd(n):=# {(c,d)\in X_n^2 : c+d is an odd square}.           (11)

Write \alpha _k := R_n(s_k-8).  Then S(n)=\sum _{k\geq 1}\alpha _k and S_odd(n)=\sum _{k odd}\alpha _k.  Observe  

  |\alpha _{k+1}-\alpha _k| \leq  s_{k+1}-s_k = 2k+1.                   (12)

Hence  

  S_odd(n)-S_even(n)=\sum _{j=1}^{\lfloor K/2\rfloor }(\alpha _{2j-1}-\alpha _{2j}), where K\approx \sqrt{2n}.  
  |\alpha _{2j-1}-\alpha _{2j}|\leq 4j-1, so

  |S_odd(n)-S_even(n)| \leq  \sum _{j\leq \sqrt{2n}} (4j) = O(n).      (13)

Because S(n)=S_odd(n)+S_even(n) we deduce  

  S_odd(n)=\frac{1}{2}S(n)+O(n)  
      = (2/3)(\sqrt{2}-1) n^{3/2}+O(n).                    (14)

Remark: the error term can in fact be sharpened to O(\sqrt{n}), but O(n) suffices for our limit.


3.  Imposing the congruence c \equiv  d (mod m)  
Fix an odd square S and set  

  I_S := {c\in X_n : S-c\in X_n}.                               (15)

I_S is an interval whose length \alpha _S:=|I_S| obeys 1\leq \alpha _S\leq n and

  \sum _{S odd square}\alpha _S = S_odd(n).                       (16)

Because m is odd, 2 is invertible modulo m.  Setting  
  c \equiv  2^{-1}S (mod m)                                   (17)
ensures (B); for each S there is exactly one residue class.  Among \alpha _S consecutive integers that class occurs either \lfloor \alpha _S/m\rfloor  or \lceil \alpha _S/m\rceil  times, i.e.

  # {c\in I_S : (B)} = \alpha _S/m + \varepsilon _S,  |\varepsilon _S|\leq 1.           (18)

Each such c determines d uniquely; since S is odd, (C) is automatic.  Summing (18) over all relevant odd squares yields

  F(n)= (1/m) S_odd(n) + E(n),  |E(n)|\leq \sum |\varepsilon _S|=O(\sqrt{n}).   (19)


4.  Asymptotics of F(n) and the limit  
Insert (14) into (19):

  F(n)= (1/m)\cdot (2/3)(\sqrt{2}-1) n^{3/2} + O(n).               (20)

Finally, by (2),

  p_n\sqrt{n} = F(n)/n^{3/2}  
      = (2/3m)(\sqrt{2}-1) + O(1/\sqrt{n}).                      (21)

Hence the limit exists and equals  

  L(m)= (2/3m)(\sqrt{2} - 1). \square 

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Multiple interacting constraints  
   The original kernel variant dealt only with the square–sum condition.  The
   enhanced version imposes two further, logically independent constraints: a
   congruence on the difference (B) and a parity requirement (C).  Their
   interaction with the square condition forces separate analyses of the even and
   odd squares, a careful use of modular arithmetic (invertibility of 2 mod m),
   and the isolation of boundary terms that were absent in the original problem.

•  Use of finer arithmetic structure  
   Unlike the original solution, which needed only a single summation over the
   square indices t, the enhanced solution must decompose that sum into even and
   odd parts, work inside individual residue classes modulo m, and solve a
   family of linear congruences to count admissible pairs.  This brings in tools
   from elementary algebraic number theory (Chinese Remainder Theorem, units
   modulo m) that never appeared in the original argument.

•  Asymptotic independence is no longer obvious  
   Showing that the extra conditions merely scale the leading term by the factor
   1⁄(2m) requires proving an approximate statistical independence between the
   “square–sum” property and simultaneity of the modular / parity constraints, an
   argument that relies on delicate error estimates (the o(1) remainders in
   steps (3) and (4)).  None of this is needed for the parent problem.

•  Deeper combinatorial bookkeeping  
   The pair–count now proceeds in three nested levels:  
  – counting solutions of c + d = S,  
  – selecting the odd-square values of S,  
  – filtering by a linear modular condition.  
   Each layer preserves or modifies the asymptotic main term in a different way,
   so the solver must keep exact track of constants through several reductions.

Because of these additional layers of modular, parity and asymptotic reasoning,
the enhanced kernel variant is substantially more intricate than both the
original AIME problem and the simpler “shifted–interval” kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an odd positive integer  
  m  (so that 2 is invertible modulo m).

For every n\in \mathbb{N} put  
  X_n := {5,6,7,\ldots ,n+4}.

Choose two integers c and d independently and uniformly at random from X_n and define  

  p_n := P[(A) c+d is a perfect square,  
      (B) c \equiv  d (mod m), and  
      (C) c and d have opposite parity].

Prove that the limit  

  L(m) := lim_{n\to \infty } (p_n \sqrt{n})

exists and that  

  L(m) = (2 / 3m)\cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1).

(For m = 1 we recover the classical constant (2/3)(\sqrt{2}-1); the modular restriction divides the constant by m, while the parity requirement removes the factor 2 that appears in the AIME 1994 problem.)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout ``pair'' means ordered pair (c,d); all implied constants are absolute.

0.  Basic reformulation  
Let  

  F(n) := # {(c,d)\in X_n^2 : (A), (B), (C) hold}.                      (1)

Because |X_n| = n,  

  p_n = F(n)/n^2  \Rightarrow   p_n\sqrt{n} = F(n)/n^{3/2}.                    (2)

Hence we need an asymptotic expansion for F(n).


1.  Pairs whose sum is a square after the +4 shift  
Write  

  S(n) := # {(c,d)\in X_n^2 : c+d is a perfect square}.             (3)

Denote, for 1\leq s\leq 2n,  

  R_n(s) := # {(u,v)\in [1,n]^2 : u+v=s}  
       = max(0, min(s-1, 2n+1-s)).                       (4)

For X_n the translation (c,d)\mapsto (c-4,d-4) sends X_n^2 bijectively onto [1,n]^2, and

  # {(c,d)\in X_n^2 : c+d=s} = R_n(s-8).                            (5)

Hence, if s_k:=k^2,

  S(n)=\sum _{k\geq 1} R_n(s_k-8).                                     (6)

Classically (AIME 1994)  

  S_0(n):=\sum _{k\geq 1} R_n(s_k)= (4/3)(\sqrt{2}-1) n^{3/2}+O(n).         (7)

We compare S(n) and S_0(n).

Fix k with s_k\leq 2n+8.  If both s_k and s_k-8 lie in [2,2n] then, because R_n is 1-Lipschitz,  

  |R_n(s_k-8)-R_n(s_k)|\leq 8.                                (8)

There are \lfloor \sqrt{2n+8}\rfloor  terms, giving a contribution O(\sqrt{n}).

If exactly one of s_k, s_k-8 lies in [2,2n], then s_k is within 8 of an endpoint of that interval; at most 16 such k occur, each contributing \leq n.  Therefore

  |S(n)-S_0(n)| = O(n).                                     (9)

Inserting (7) we obtain  

  S(n)= (4/3)(\sqrt{2}-1) n^{3/2}+O(n).                        (10)


2.  Keeping only odd squares - parity filter (C)  
Let  

  S_odd(n):=# {(c,d)\in X_n^2 : c+d is an odd square}.           (11)

Write \alpha _k := R_n(s_k-8).  Then S(n)=\sum _{k\geq 1}\alpha _k and S_odd(n)=\sum _{k odd}\alpha _k.  Observe  

  |\alpha _{k+1}-\alpha _k| \leq  s_{k+1}-s_k = 2k+1.                   (12)

Hence  

  S_odd(n)-S_even(n)=\sum _{j=1}^{\lfloor K/2\rfloor }(\alpha _{2j-1}-\alpha _{2j}), where K\approx \sqrt{2n}.  
  |\alpha _{2j-1}-\alpha _{2j}|\leq 4j-1, so

  |S_odd(n)-S_even(n)| \leq  \sum _{j\leq \sqrt{2n}} (4j) = O(n).      (13)

Because S(n)=S_odd(n)+S_even(n) we deduce  

  S_odd(n)=\frac{1}{2}S(n)+O(n)  
      = (2/3)(\sqrt{2}-1) n^{3/2}+O(n).                    (14)

Remark: the error term can in fact be sharpened to O(\sqrt{n}), but O(n) suffices for our limit.


3.  Imposing the congruence c \equiv  d (mod m)  
Fix an odd square S and set  

  I_S := {c\in X_n : S-c\in X_n}.                               (15)

I_S is an interval whose length \alpha _S:=|I_S| obeys 1\leq \alpha _S\leq n and

  \sum _{S odd square}\alpha _S = S_odd(n).                       (16)

Because m is odd, 2 is invertible modulo m.  Setting  
  c \equiv  2^{-1}S (mod m)                                   (17)
ensures (B); for each S there is exactly one residue class.  Among \alpha _S consecutive integers that class occurs either \lfloor \alpha _S/m\rfloor  or \lceil \alpha _S/m\rceil  times, i.e.

  # {c\in I_S : (B)} = \alpha _S/m + \varepsilon _S,  |\varepsilon _S|\leq 1.           (18)

Each such c determines d uniquely; since S is odd, (C) is automatic.  Summing (18) over all relevant odd squares yields

  F(n)= (1/m) S_odd(n) + E(n),  |E(n)|\leq \sum |\varepsilon _S|=O(\sqrt{n}).   (19)


4.  Asymptotics of F(n) and the limit  
Insert (14) into (19):

  F(n)= (1/m)\cdot (2/3)(\sqrt{2}-1) n^{3/2} + O(n).               (20)

Finally, by (2),

  p_n\sqrt{n} = F(n)/n^{3/2}  
      = (2/3m)(\sqrt{2}-1) + O(1/\sqrt{n}).                      (21)

Hence the limit exists and equals  

  L(m)= (2/3m)(\sqrt{2} - 1). \square 

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Multiple interacting constraints  
   The original kernel variant dealt only with the square–sum condition.  The
   enhanced version imposes two further, logically independent constraints: a
   congruence on the difference (B) and a parity requirement (C).  Their
   interaction with the square condition forces separate analyses of the even and
   odd squares, a careful use of modular arithmetic (invertibility of 2 mod m),
   and the isolation of boundary terms that were absent in the original problem.

•  Use of finer arithmetic structure  
   Unlike the original solution, which needed only a single summation over the
   square indices t, the enhanced solution must decompose that sum into even and
   odd parts, work inside individual residue classes modulo m, and solve a
   family of linear congruences to count admissible pairs.  This brings in tools
   from elementary algebraic number theory (Chinese Remainder Theorem, units
   modulo m) that never appeared in the original argument.

•  Asymptotic independence is no longer obvious  
   Showing that the extra conditions merely scale the leading term by the factor
   1⁄(2m) requires proving an approximate statistical independence between the
   “square–sum” property and simultaneity of the modular / parity constraints, an
   argument that relies on delicate error estimates (the o(1) remainders in
   steps (3) and (4)).  None of this is needed for the parent problem.

•  Deeper combinatorial bookkeeping  
   The pair–count now proceeds in three nested levels:  
  – counting solutions of c + d = S,  
  – selecting the odd-square values of S,  
  – filtering by a linear modular condition.  
   Each layer preserves or modifies the asymptotic main term in a different way,
   so the solver must keep exact track of constants through several reductions.

Because of these additional layers of modular, parity and asymptotic reasoning,
the enhanced kernel variant is substantially more intricate than both the
original AIME problem and the simpler “shifted–interval” kernel variant.
